coredata.dc:title: N/S co-doped carbon dots-based solution-gated graphene transistors (SGGT) for ultra-sensitive and selective determination of Hg (II) ions in real water and serum samples
coredata.dc:creator: Xue, Chenglong
Ren, Zhanpeng
Wang, Jianying
Deng, Minghua
Li, Jiashen
Wang, Xianbao
Li, Jinhua
coredata.dc:description: The conventional methods for detecting Hg2+ ions are generally impaired by low sensitivity, a high limit of detection (LOD), and the long detection time required. In this work, we develop a novel solution gated graphene transistor (SGGT) based on carbon dots doped with N and S elements (N/S CDs) as gate electrode probes. The prepared N/S CDs, which are 3–5 nm in diameter, are first modified at the gate of the SGGT through covalent bonding that specifically recognizes Hg2+ ions. The SGGT is then used as a Hg2+ sensor, exhibiting good selectivity, ultrahigh sensitivity, good reproducibility, and a short detection time. The SGGT sensor has an excellent linear detection range, from 0.1 fM to 1 nM. The LOD can reach 28 aM, which is far lower than that of conventional detection methods. Moreover, the SGGT sensor also has a rapid response time of ∼ 40 s. Finally, the ability to analyze real water samples and serum samples is demonstrated.
coredata.dcterms:subject: SGGT
Carbon dots
Hg2+ ions
Detection
Sensitivity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Gate electrode modification with N/S CDs
Synthesis and characterization of N/S CDs
Detection of Hg2+ ions by SGGT
The SGGT sensing mechanism for Hg2+ ions
Analysis of real water samples for Hg2+ ions using the SGGT
Detection of Hg2+ ions in serum samples using the SGGT sensor

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by Knowledge Innovation Program of Wuhan Science and Technology Bureau (Grant No. 2023020201010139), the Opening Project of Key Laboratory of Organosilicon Chemistry and Material Technology of Ministry of Education, Hangzhou Normal University ( KFJJ2022008 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 51673060 ), Science and Technology Department of Hubei province (Grant No. 2020BIB020 ), the Opening Project of Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Chemical Materials and Devices of Ministry of Education, Jianghan University ( JDGD-202205 ), and the Ministry of Science, Overseas Expertise Introduction Center for Discipline Innovation ( D18025 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The gate, source, and drain preparation of the SGGT device. (b) A single layer of graphene was transferred to the channel area. (c) Cysteamine was used to modify the surface of the gate electrode. (d) N/S CDs modified the gate surface. (e) Hg2+ ions were captured by the N/S CDs-modified gate electrode.

(a) TEM and HRTEM images of N/S CDs. (b) The absorption, excitation, and fluorescence spectra of N/S CDs at the excitation wavelength of 320 nm. (c) FTIR spectrum of N/S CDs. (d) Full-range XPS survey spectrum of N/S CDs.

(a) Selectivity measurements of the SGGT sensor. Ten metal ions, (Ba2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Al3+, Fe3+, and Hg2+) were tested. The concentration of all ions was fixed at 1.0 × 10−7 M. (b) The bar graph of |ΔI D| for different metal ions. The |ΔI D| is the absolute value of current response changes. (c) The response of the channel current (I D) to an increased in of Hg2+ ion concentrations in PBS solution was measured at V D = 0.1 V and V G = 0.3 V. (d) |ΔI D| of the SGGT vs the logarithm of the concentration of Hg2+ ions.

(a) Schematic diagram of variation in EDL thickness near the gate electrode before and after the capture of Hg2+ ions. (b) The schematic of the potential between the gate and channel. (c) The capacitance of the sensor versus the frequency before and after the capture of Hg2+ ions.
(a) Transfer curves of the sensor measured in PBS before and after the addition of different concentrations of Hg2+ ions. (b) The linear relationship of ΔV Dirac with the concentration of Hg2+ ions.

(a) The I D-V G curves measured in PBS solution before and after the addition of different concentrations of Hg2+ ions in serum samples. (b) The linear relationship of |ΔV Dirac| with the concentration of Hg2+ ions in serum samples. (c) The I D-V G curves measured in PBS solution before and after the addition of different metal ions in serum samples. (d) Bar graph of |ΔV Dirac| for different metal ions in serum samples.
Determination of Hg2+ ions spiked into real water samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: N/S co-doped carbon dots-based solution-gated graphene transistors (SGGT) for ultra-sensitive and selective determination of Hg (II) ions in real water and serum samples

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contribute to this work equally.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The conventional methods for detecting Hg2+ ions are generally impaired by low sensitivity, a high limit of detection (LOD), and the long detection time required. In this work, we develop a novel solution gated graphene transistor (SGGT) based on carbon dots doped with N and S elements (N/S CDs) as gate electrode probes. The prepared N/S CDs, which are 3–5 nm in diameter, are first modified at the gate of the SGGT through covalent bonding that specifically recognizes Hg2+ ions. The SGGT is then used as a Hg2+ sensor, exhibiting good selectivity, ultrahigh sensitivity, good reproducibility, and a short detection time. The SGGT sensor has an excellent linear detection range, from 0.1 fM to 1 nM. The LOD can reach 28 aM, which is far lower than that of conventional detection methods. Moreover, the SGGT sensor also has a rapid response time of ∼ 40 s. Finally, the ability to analyze real water samples and serum samples is demonstrated.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metal pollution, from both natural and industrial sources, poses a great challenge for environmental protection and human health [1,2]. After heavy metal ions are dissolved in water, they readily accumulate and enter the food chain [3], ultimately causing severe harm to human health [4]. Hg2+ is one of the most toxic heavy metal ions because water-soluble Hg2+ ions can be converted to more toxic organic mercury compounds that can easily enter the ecosystem [5,6]. Owing to their non-biodegradability and bioaccumulation [7], even low concentration of Hg2+ ions can cause many diseases, causing harm to the liver, kidney [8], nervous and immune systems, etc [9].

Various detection methods are currently used to sense Hg2+ ions. Conventional detection methods include colorimetry [10], fluorimetry [11,12], atomic absorption spectroscopy [13], inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectroscopy, and electrochemical detection [14,15]. These methods often need professional operators, expensive detection equipment, and a long detection cycle. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop a method the detection of Hg2+ ions detection that has good sensitivity, high selectivity, and a short response time.

To resolve the aforementioned issues, the solution-gated graphene transistors (SGGTs) have been used in chemical sensors. They have many advantages, including a low limit of detection (LOD), high selectivity, short response time, real-time monitoring, and low operating voltages (below 1 V) [16–19]. Various SGGT sensors, including ions [16], thrombin [20], and DNA [21,22], have been developed in the past decade [23]. Our group recently reported the development of a novel type of carbon dots (CDs)-modified SGGT to realize the detection of Cu2+ [24] and Fe3+ ions [25]. Our group synthesized –NH2 and –COOH-rich carbon quantum dots (CQDs) using sodium alginate and ethylenediamine as raw materials and the CQDs modified onto the gate electrode of SGGT can well recognize the Cu2+ ions. Additionally, we also prepared –NH2 and –COOH-rich CQDs using lignosulfonate sodium and p-phenylenediamine as the raw materials. The sensor with the CQDs modified gate can real the detection of Fe3+ ions. CDs are a type luminescent carbon nanoparticles (size < 10 nm) [26,27]. CDs offer great promise for applications in both optical imaging and analytical fields because they are eco-friendly, biocompatibe, and cost-effective, with low toxicity, unique optical properties, and tolerance for a wide range of functional groups [27–29]. More importantly, the CDs have the ability to effectively detect various ions and biomolecules in a solution via fluorescence-based method [30–33]. From our results, we find that, compared with fluorescence detection using CDs solution, the SGGT sensors have higher performances, e.g., ultralow LOD, probably coming from the good signal amplification function when CDs are used as gate probes. Although progress has been made, expanding the detection ability of SGGT sensors for some of most toxic heavy metal ions, e.g., Hg2+ ions, and understanding the function of CDs in SGGT sensors remain a challenge.

In this study, we prepared a new SGGT sensor to realize the highly sensitive and highly selective detection Hg2+ ions. The CDs are synthesized by a hydrothermal method using glutathione and sodium citrate as C, N, and S sources [34]. The obtained N/S CDs emit blue light and have many amino, carboxyl, and thiol groups on their surface, which can effectively capture the targets Hg2+ ions. After the capture of the Hg2+ ions, the capacitance of the electric double layer (EDL) near the gate electrode changes, causing a response in the transfer curve [24]. The transfer curve shift or the induced channel current can be measured, finally determining the concentrations of Hg2+ ions. The obtained sensor exhibits the good sensitivity and selectivity for Hg2+ ions. The sensor has a good linear detection range which is from 0.1 fM to 1 nM, and an ultralow LOD of 0.1 fM is achieved. Moreover, the response time of the sensor to Hg2+ ions at 1 fM (1 × 10−15 M) is ∼ 40 s, meeting the practical requirement for real-time monitoring. Finally, Hg2+ detection in real water and serum samples has been demonstrated using the SGGT sensor.

Acetone, ethanol, ferric chloride, barium nitrate, zinc nitrate, cobalt nitrate, lead nitrate, manganese nitrate, copper nitrate, nickel nitrate, aluminum nitrate, iron nitrate, and mercury nitrate were purchased from Sinopharm Co. Ltd. (Beijing, China). Sodium citrate, glutathione, poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA), poly(dimethylsiloxane) (PDMS), 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), cysteamine, and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were purchased from Aladdin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Phosphate-buffered saline (1 × PBS) solution (pH = 7.2) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). Ultrapure deionized (DI) water used in the experiment was obtained from a Millipore water purification system. A glass substrate was purchased from Guluo Glass Co. Ltd. (Luoyang, China). The CVD single graphene layer grown on copper foil was purchased from Sixcarbon tech Company. (Shenzhen, China).

The N/S CDs were prepared following a previously described report [34]. Briefly, sodium citrate (0.032 g) and glutathione (0.11 g) were first dissolved in deionized water (3.5 mL) and placed into a Teflon-lined autoclave (50 mL). Then, the autoclave was placed in an oven (180 ℃) for reaction. After 4.5 h, a brownish solution was obtained. The product was centrifuged at 12000 rpm/min, filtered through a 2 μm filter membranes, and dialyzed (molecular weight cut-off: 1000 Da) for 72 h, finally, the yellow CDs solution was obtained.

First, the glass substrate was subject to ultrasonic cleaning with acetone, anhydrous ethanol, and deionized water sequentially (20 mins each time), and then placed in a drying oven for drying. Chromium (Cr) and gold (Au) were vaporized using a thermal evaporation for the coating system (JSD-300). Cr and Au were deposited on the surface of the glass substrate to form the gate (G), source (S), and drain (D) electrodes (Cr: 13 nm/Au: 120 nm), as shown in Fig. 1
a. The channel area between the source and the drain was then covered by a single layer of graphene using a method based on polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA) film transfer, as shown in Fig. 1 b. The PMMA film transfer method was as follows: first, 100 µL of PMMA (35 mg/mL) was spin-coated on the surface of CVD-grown graphene (copper substrate), and the PMMA/graphene film was heated at 100 °C for 10 min to solidify the PMMA. The CVD-grown graphene/Cu foil was treated with FeCl3 solution for etching; after 3 h, the Cu foil was considered completely etched. The PMMA/graphene film (0.2 cm × 0.6 cm) was transferred onto the surface of the source and drain electrodes. After thermal treatment (60 °C for 10 mins, followed by 100 °C for 30 mins), the PMMA/graphene film was fixed on the channel. Finally, the PMMA layer was removed using acetone and the SGGT device was washed with deionized water. The Raman spectrum identified single-layer graphene on a silicon substrate, as shown in Fig. S1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Gate electrode modification with N/S CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The gate electrode of SGGT was first modified by 10 μL cysteamine (5 mM) for 10 h (under low temperatures and dark conditions) (Fig. 1 c) [35]. The carboxyl groups of N/S CDs were then activated by EDC/NHS treatment for 1 h [35]. Subsequently, PBS buffer was used to wash the cysteamine-modified gold gate electrode many times and the activated N/S CDs solution was added to the electrode surface to undergo a coupling reaction, as shown in Fig. 1 d. Finally, PBS buffer was used to wash the electrode many times to remove the unfixed CDs. Thus, the CDs-modified SGGT for Hg2+ detection was obtained, as shown in Fig. 1 e and Fig. S2 .

First, 3.25 g of mercuric nitrate was dissolved in 100 mL of water to produce a 0.1 M solution of mercuric nitrate. Then, by gradually diluting the 0.1 M solution of mercuric nitrate, mercuric nitrate solutions with a concentration of 10−7 M, 10−9 M, 10−10 M, 10−11 M, 10−12 M, 10−13 M, 10−14 M, 10−15 M, and 10−16 M were obtained. The same method can also be used to produce solutions of other metal ions with a concentration of 10−7 M.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of N/S CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were used to characterize the synthesized N/S CDs, as shown in Fig. 2
a. The N/S CDs possess sphere-like nanoparticles with diameters of approximately ∼ 3 to 5 nm. The HRTEM image shows that the N/S CDs have a good crystalline structure and the lattice spacing is ∼ 0.19 nm. The lattice spacing is attributed to the (100) plane of graphite [36]. This result further indicates that the N/S CDs are crystalline CQDs, similar to the previous report [36]. Fig. 2 b shows the absorption, excitation, and emission spectra of N/S CDs following excitation with UV light at 320 nm. The results show that the strongest absorption peak and excitation wavelength are located at ∼ 320 nm. The excitation of 320 nm results in emission at 410 nm, appearing as blue fluorescence, as shown in the inset of Fig. 2 b.

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were collected to determine the functional groups of the N/S CDs ( Fig. 2 c). The peaks at 3398 cm−1 and 2983 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching of O-H and N-H bonds, respectively [37]. The C = O stretching corresponds to the peak at 1637 cm−1 [37]. The chemical composition of N/S CDs was determined from the X-ray photoelectron spectrum (XPS). As shown in Fig. 2 d, five peaks were located at 531.25, 497.4, 398.77, 284.22, and 167.56 eV, respectively. Correspondingly, this shows that the N/S CDs contain oxygen (O 1 s (40.56 %)), sodium (Na 1 s (35.02 %)), nitrogen (N 1 s (3.2 %)), sulfur (S 2p (1.52 %)) and carbon elements (C 1 s (19.7 %)), respectively [34]. The high-resolution spectra of the elements were also collected. The C 1 s spectrum has three Gaussian peaks (Fig. S3
a) at 284.8, 286.2 and 288.2 eV, respectively. This indicates the present of C-N/C-O, C = O/C-N, and –COO groups, respectively [37]. The O 1 s spectrum has three peaks at 531.07, 531.99, and 535.72 eV, respectively. The C-O, C = O, and C = O/C-O-C/–COO groups were confirmed (Fig. S3 b) [28]. The N 1 s spectrum shows the presence of C-N, C-N-C, and N-H groups (Fig. S3 c) located at 399.9, 398.9, and 401.3 eV, respectively [38]. Finally, the sulfur peaks at 164.2, 168.2, and 169.52 eV, indicate that the N/S CDs contain C-S and C-S-C bonds (Fig. S3 d) [11]. The results confirm that the N/S CDs contain N and S elements and also have –COOH and –NH2 on their surfaces [34], this is important for their modification to Au gate electrodes and as probes for the detection of Hg2+ ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg2+ ions by SGGT
ce:section.ce:para:
To demonstrate the selectivity for Hg2+ ions, 10 of metal ions (Ba2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Al3+, Fe3+, and Hg2+) were tested. The concentrations of the ten metal ions were fixed at 10−7 M. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the current response to Hg2+ ions was largest. The |ΔI D| to the Hg2+ ions was at least six times larger than those of other metal ions, as shown in Fig. 3 b. The SGGT sensor appears to have excellent selectivity for Hg2+ ions among the 10 of metal ions tested. As the N/S CDs are used as gate probes, the selectivity probably arises from the specific interaction between Hg2+ ions and CDs surface groups, which was confirmed by the detection of luminescent quenching of N/S CDs solution induced by Hg2+ ions, as shown in Fig. S4
[34]. Fig. S5
a and
Fig. S5
b show the SEM images before and after the addition of Hg2+ ions. It can be observed that the morphology of the gate electrode surface with the addition of Hg2+ ions is different from that of the gate electrode surface without the addition of Hg2+ ions, indicating that the target Hg2+ ions are captured by N/S CDs on the gate electrode after Hg2+ ions addition. Further, the results of the SEM element mapping in Fig. S5
c and
Fig. S5
d clearly shows that the Hg2+ ions are well captured to by N/S CDs on the gate surface after the detection of Hg2+ ions. It is clear that the surface of gate electrode is covered by a lot of Hg element after the addition of Hg2+ ions. Hower, Hg element is not measured on the surface of the gate electrode when the Hg2+ ions are not added. The Hg elements were observed on the gate electrode surface after addition. This indicates that Hg elements aggregate on the gate electrode surface after addition of Hg2+ ions because they are captured by N/S CDs on the gate electrode surface. These results provide further evidence that the prepared N/S CDs can effectively capture Hg2+ ions and can therefore be used as a probe for the detection of Hg2+ ions in the SGGT sensor. Further, to determine the sensitivity and linear detection range of the sensors for Hg2+ ions, the time vs current curve was measured for different Hg2+ ions concentrations and presented in Fig. 3 c. As the concentration of Hg2+ ions increased from 0.1 fM to 1 nM, the I D increased from 325.65 to 336.76 μA gradationally at the voltage of V D = 0.1 V. Furthermore, the |ΔI D| has a good linear response to the concentration of Hg2+ ions, as shown in Fig. 3 d. The relationship was linear in the range from 0.1 fM to 1 nM and an ultralow LOD of 0.1 fM was obtained. The linear equation Y = 1.552X + 25.455 was obtained from fitting to the curve and the correlation coefficient R 2 was 0.996. Importantly, the response time to Hg2+ ions at 0.1 pM (1 × 10−15 M) is approximately 40 s, meeting the practical requirement for real-time monitoring. To confirm effect of the N/S CDs on the detection of Hg2+ ions, a control experiment was conducted, as shown in Fig. S6
a, S6b. Without the modification of N/S CDs, there was no obvious response of SGGT to the Hg2+ ions. This indicates that the N/S CDs play a key role in the detection of Hg2+ ions. Fig. S7 shows the stability of sensors. The results show almost no change in the transfer curve over 8 h. This good stability is beneficial to the real-world application of the sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The SGGT sensing mechanism for Hg2+ ions
ce:section.ce:para:
The detection mechanism was investigated further. In Fig. 4
a, a schematic diagram of the variation in the thickness of the EDL near the gate before/after the capture of Hg2+ ions is presented. When Hg2+ ions are added dropwise into the electrolyte solution, they are chelated with the N/S CDs fixed to the gate electrode to form a stable cationic-CDs chelation structure. The cationic-CDs chelation structure is positively charged. Therefore, it will attract the negative ions in the test solution, and in turn repulse the positive charge from shifting toward the gate. Thus, a new charge balance will be formed in the system. This process may lead to an increase in space of the gate electrode and the charge caused by the electrostatic field. This means that the thickness of the EDL at the side of the gate will increase, leading to a corresponding decrease in the capacitance [24].

Total capacitance can be written as [25]
(1)
1
C
total
=
1
C
G - E
+
1
C
E - C

where Ctotal is the total capacitance of the sensor, CG-E is the capacitance of EDL1 and CE-C is the capacitance of EDL2, as shown in Fig. 4 b. The decrease in C G−E leads to a decrease in C total. Therefore, the potential is redistributed. The I D of a SGGT can be written as
(2)
I D
≈
W L
μ
C i
V G
-
V
Dirac
-
V D
2
·
V D
|
V G
-
V
Dirac
| > >
V D

where W and L are the width and the length of the channel length, μ is the carrier mobility of graphene (electron/hole), C i is the total capacitance, and V Dirac is the voltage at the Dirac point [21]. The increase in thickness of the EDL1 will induce a decrease in C i. Therefore, the transfer curve moves toward the positive gate voltage. To confirm the capacitance change induced by the capture of Hg2+ ions, the capacitance of the sensor was measured before/after the capture of Hg2+ ions. Fig. 4 c shows the relationship between the capacitance and the frequency before/after the capture of Hg2+ ions. The capacitance of the sensor decreases after the addition of Hg2+ ions. The result also further validates our detection mechanism. As shown in Fig. 5
a, the transfer curve shifts toward the positive gate voltage as the Hg2+ concentration increases. The results confirm our proposed hypothesis. The ΔV Dirac values can be extracted from the transfer curves of the device constructed for different concentrations of Hg2+ ions, as shown in Fig. 5 b. It can be seen that ΔV Dirac and the concentration of Hg2+ ions are linearly related and maintain a simple quantitative relationship. The concentration of Hg2+ ions can be quantitatively calculated from their linear relationship. The LOD can be calculated by the equation (3) [39]:
(3)
LOD =
3 Δ
V Dirac
noise
- a
b

where ΔV noise is the average response of the blank sample, a and b are the intercept and slope of the calibration linear relation, respectively. As shown in Fig. S8 , ΔV noise of the SGGT sensor, based on three measurements, was 0.02 V. From the formula Y = 22.26X + 368.25 of the correlation in Fig. 5 b, a = 368.25 and b = 22.26. From equation (3), as X = Lg C, the LOD is 2.8 × 10−17 M, which is better than for previous detection methods (Table S1), indicating that the sensor has good sensitivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of real water samples for Hg2+ ions using the SGGT
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the proposed strategy, the SGGT was applied to examine real water samples. Three real water samples, e.g., lake water, river water, and tap water, were selected. The lake water was from the Sand Lake, the river water was from the Yangtze River, and the tap water was from Hubei University in Wuhan City. These samples were first treated by centrifugation, then filtration, and finally digested with nitric acid. Different concentrations of Hg2+ ions (0.1 nM, 1 nM, 10 nM) were added to the treated water samples, as shown in Fig. S9 . As shown in Table 1 , the recovery rate range was from ∼ 88.67 % to 102.33 % when used to analyze the real samples, indicating that the SGGT sensor can quantitatively measure Hg2 + ions in real water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg2+ ions in serum samples using the SGGT sensor
ce:section.ce:para:
Finally, the SGGT sensor was utilized to measure the concentrations of Hg2+ ions in artificially spiked serum samples. Fig. 6
a shows the I D-V G curves of the sensors for the serum samples spiked with different concentrations of Hg2+ ions. As the concentration of Hg2+ ions in the serum samples increases, V Dirac shifts toward the positive gate voltage direction. This is consistent with the previous results of Hg2+ ion detection in the absence of serum samples (Fig. 5). Additionally, |ΔV Dirac| exhibits an excellent linear relationship with the concentration of Hg2+ ions in the serum samples (R 2 = 0.994), as depicted in Fig. 6 b. The high selectivity of the SGGT sensor toward Hg2+ ions is shown in Fig. 6 c. Ten simulated serum samples containing different metal ions, Ba2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Al3+, Fe3+, and Hg2+, were chosen for the comparison of response levels. The |ΔV Dirac| value of the Hg2+ ions is the serum sample was at least 7.4 times higher than that of the other nine metal ion serum samples, as shown in Fig. 6 d. These results indicate that the SGGT sensor exhibits excellent selectivity toward Hg2+ ions in the artificially spiked serum samples. This suggests that serum does not significantly affect the detection of Hg2+ ions by the SGGT sensor. Thus, the SGGT sensor for Hg2+ ions has good selectivity, a low LOD, a broad linear detection range (from 0.1 fM to 1 nM), and is suitable for the analysis of real water and serum samples.

In summary, a N/S co-doped CD is prepared and is used as a new SGGT sensor for the detection of Hg2+ ions. The N/S CDs is modified on the gate surface of the sensor as the probe of the Hg2+ ions. The sensors show excellent selectivity and high sensitivity. The SGGT sensor achieves a broad linear detection range, from 0.1 fM to 1 nM and an ultralow LOD of 28 aM, compared with performances in other work (Table S1). The SGGT sensor had rapid detection of ∼ 40 s and good stability. Remarkably, the quantitative detection of Hg2+ ions in real water and serum samples have been realized, indicating great potential for practical applications in the field of environmental detection and human health monitoring.

Chenglong Xue: Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation, Data curation. Zhanpeng Ren: Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation, Data curation. Jianying Wang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Minghua Deng: Formal analysis. Jiashen Li: Investigation. Xianbao Wang: Supervision. Jinhua Li: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Knowledge Innovation Program of Wuhan Science and Technology Bureau (Grant No. 2023020201010139), the Opening Project of Key Laboratory of Organosilicon Chemistry and Material Technology of Ministry of Education, Hangzhou Normal University (KFJJ2022008), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 51673060), Science and Technology Department of Hubei province (Grant No. 2020BIB020), the Opening Project of Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Chemical Materials and Devices of Ministry of Education, Jianghan University (JDGD-202205), and the Ministry of Science, Overseas Expertise Introduction Center for Discipline Innovation (D18025).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.110626.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
